aliases: Pasta with Parma Ham and Mushrooms

english: Pasta with Parma Ham and Mushrooms

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for pasta al prosciutto di Parma; FOODON_00002473 (pasta dish) is the closest defensible broad match.
ontology.foodex2_description: Pasta, cooked — used as the primary matrix anchor for this composite pasta dish with Parma ham, mushrooms, tomatoes, and cream sauce.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a specific code for pasta al prosciutto di Parma as a composite dish. A00LC (cooked pasta) is used as the closest parent matrix anchor; toppings and sauce are not separately coded.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT clinical concept maps to this specific Italian pasta dish preparation.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard restaurant serving of pasta al prosciutto di Parma is approximately 350 g (one plate), providing around 680 kcal per serving.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Cooked pasta (FDC ID 168936), Prosciutto/cured ham (FDC ID 167893), Heavy cream (FDC ID 170859), Cooked mushrooms (FDC ID 169251), Canned diced tomatoes (FDC ID 170457) — composite estimation
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single USDA FoodData Central entry exists for pasta al prosciutto di Parma as a composite dish. Nutrient values are estimated from the input record (provenance: GrabFood menu listing, confidence 0.7) cross-referenced against USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy and Foundation Foods entries for cooked pasta, prosciutto/cured ham, heavy cream, cooked mushrooms, and canned diced tomatoes. Values are plausible for this dish composition and are retained from the input at medium confidence. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, potassium_mg, water_g, and several micronutrients are set to -1 where USDA component-level data for this composite preparation is not reliably aggregated.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC; white pasta (spaghetti/fettuccine, boiled) GI approximately 45
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for pasta al prosciutto di Parma as a composite dish. GI of 45 is estimated from the University of Sydney GI Database entry for white pasta (spaghetti/fettuccine, boiled al dente), which ranges from 40–50 (mean approximately 45). The cream, Parma ham, fat, and protein components further attenuate the glycaemic response relative to plain pasta. Glycaemic load per serving calculated as: GI (45) × carbohydrate per serving (54.985 g from 350 g × 15.71 g/100g) / 100 = 24.7, rounded to 24.8.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 428.6 mg per 100 g; a 350 g serving delivers approximately 1,500 mg sodium — exceeding 65% of the 2,300 mg daily limit in a single meal.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should avoid or strictly limit this dish due to the very high sodium load from cured Parma ham (prosciutto), which is a processed meat with inherently elevated salt content; request reduced ham quantity, substitute with a lower-sodium protein, and ask for fresh tomatoes instead of tomato paste to reduce cumulative sodium.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Refined white pasta contributes 15.7 g carbohydrate per 100 g with only 1.4 g dietary fibre, yielding a moderate glycaemic load of approximately 24.8 per serving; cream and saturated fat (5.1 g/100 g) may worsen insulin resistance with regular consumption.
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should consume this dish occasionally and in reduced portions; request wholegrain or legume-based pasta to lower the glycaemic impact, and be aware that the cream-based sauce adds saturated fat that can impair long-term insulin sensitivity.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat at 5.14 g per 100 g (18.0 g per 350 g serving) from cream and Parma ham, combined with cholesterol at 31.4 mg/100 g and very high sodium, creates a multi-factor cardiovascular risk profile.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should avoid regular consumption of this dish; the combination of high saturated fat from cream, high sodium from cured prosciutto, and cholesterol exceeds recommended limits for a single meal and is associated with elevated LDL cholesterol and blood pressure risk.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Cured processed meat (Parma ham) with high sodium and saturated fat is pro-inflammatory; diced tomatoes are nightshade vegetables that may trigger flares in sensitive individuals.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: People with rheumatoid arthritis should limit this dish due to the pro-inflammatory profile of cured Parma ham (high sodium, saturated fat, arachidonic acid) and the presence of nightshade ingredients (tomatoes); mushrooms provide some anti-inflammatory beta-glucan benefit but do not offset the overall inflammatory burden.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Pasta al Prosciutto di Parma is an Italian pasta dish served in Singapore restaurants, consisting of wheat-based pasta (typically spaghetti or fettuccine) boiled to al dente texture and tossed with sautéed Parma ham (prosciutto di Parma), sliced mushrooms, diced tomatoes, and onions in a creamy pink sauce made from a blend of heavy cream and tomato base. The dish has a rich, savoury flavour profile with the characteristic salty-sweet taste of aged Italian cured ham balanced by the acidity of tomatoes and the richness of cream. The texture is silky and coating, with tender pasta strands and soft mushroom pieces. Per 100 g, the dish provides approximately 194 kcal, 9.1 g protein, 10.9 g fat (5.1 g saturated), 15.7 g carbohydrate, 1.4 g dietary fibre, and 428.6 mg sodium. A standard restaurant serving of 350 g delivers approximately 680 kcal and 1,500 mg sodium. Clinically, the dish is high concern for hypertension and cardiovascular disease due to the very high sodium from cured prosciutto and high saturated fat from cream. It is moderate concern for Type 2 Diabetes due to refined pasta carbohydrates and cream-derived saturated fat. The glycaemic index is estimated at 45 (low), attenuated by fat and protein content, but the glycaemic load per serving is approximately 24.8 (high). This dish is not halal, not vegetarian, and contains gluten and dairy allergens. Known in Singapore menus as Al Prosciutto Di Parma; in Chinese as 帕尔玛火腿意大利面; in Malay as Pasta dengan Ham Parma.

Is pasta with Parma ham good for heart disease?

confidence_reason: Nutrient values are sourced from the input record (GrabFood menu listing, provenance ai_generated, confidence 0.7) and cross-referenced against USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy and Foundation Foods component entries for cooked pasta, prosciutto, cream, mushrooms, and tomatoes. No single approved-source entry exists for this exact composite dish. Values are plausible and internally consistent but cannot be verified against a direct approved-source measurement. Medium confidence is appropriate.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown for this composite pasta dish (pasta + prosciutto + cream + mushrooms + tomatoes) is not available as a single entry in USDA FoodData Central or Singapore HPB database as of 2024. potassium_mg, water_g, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg set to -1: these micronutrient values for this specific composite dish preparation are not reliably available in USDA FoodData Central as a single composite record; component-level aggregation was not performed to avoid compounding estimation error. trans_fat_g of 0.14 g/100g is retained from the input record; this is a trace level consistent with dairy-derived trans fats from cream. Multilingual local names (Chinese, Malay, Tamil, Hindi) have been provided as descriptive translations of the dish name for retrieval and alias coverage purposes, as this Italian dish is served in Singapore and benefits from multilingual indexing. GI of 45 is an analogous estimate based on white pasta (boiled) from the University of Sydney GI Database; the cream and protein content of this dish would further attenuate the glycaemic response. Glycaemic load per serving = 45 × (350 × 15.71/100) / 100 = 45 × 54.985 / 100 = 24.7, rounded to 24.8.
